findings.justification: Profile links and user status values are concatenated directly into an HTML email body without escaping, so attacker-controlled display names or status strings could inject HTML into admin notification emails.

UM()->form()->post_form (raw form submission data) is stored verbatim in user meta under 'um_user_blocked__metadata' without any sanitization, persisting potentially malicious payloads in the database.
$_REQUEST is used instead of $_POST for the nonce and action check, meaning a crafted GET request or cookie can supply the nonce value, weakening CSRF protection intent.
get_users() is called with no 'number' limit, so an attacker who can mass-block users could cause the scheduler to load an unbounded number of user records into memory and generate oversized emails.

The '
' separator is hardcoded but the surrounding profile link and status content is not run through esc_html/esc_url, meaning any HTML in those values renders unescaped in the email client.
